key_insight: Glassy polymers yield and neck because stretching increases molecular mobility, causing a transition from a frozen to an unfrozen shear state (“shear unfreezing”).
engineering_relevance: The theory gives a unified, physics-based way to predict yield stress, plasticity, and neck initiation versus strain rate and temperature for polymer design and processing.
method: Build a minimal constitutive model from the Doolittle volume-dependent relaxation time.
Derive the flow law with the Onsager variational principle.
Use the model to obtain analytical yield stress and a necking phase diagram.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports analytical predictions and phase diagrams that explain yielding, plasticity, and neck initiation under constant strain-rate loading, but does not mention experimental validation.
watch_item: Because the framework is minimal and theory-driven, its predictive accuracy may depend on how well the assumed relaxation-time law captures real polymer chemistry and temperature/strain-rate regimes.
paper_title: Shear Unfreezing Explains Yielding, Plasticity and Neck Initiation of Glassy Polymers
